name: Pazuzu
description: Pazuzu is a demon king of the wind, and the son of the god Hanbi, in Assyrian and Babylonian mythology. He is depicted with a terrifying appearance: a human body, the head of a lion or dog, eagle-like talons for feet, two pairs of wings, a scorpion's tail, and a serpentine phallus. Despite his demonic nature, Pazuzu was also believed to be a protector against evil winds and diseases, particularly those brought by his rival, the demon Lamashtu, who threatened pregnant women and infants. He was often invoked for protection against these malevolent forces, and amulets bearing his image were commonly worn or placed in homes. His dual nature reflects the complex understanding of good and evil in ancient Mesopotamian beliefs, where even demonic entities could be harnessed for benevolent purposes. Pazuzu's power resided in his control over the winds, allowing him to both unleash destructive storms and ward off harmful influences. He was a complex figure, embodying both the threat of chaos and the promise of protection, deeply intertwined with the lives of the ancient Mesopotamians. His grotesque imagery served as a visual deterrent to evil spirits, reminding them of the power that could be invoked against them.
subtitle: King of the Wind Demons

keyMyths.title: Pazuzu and Lamashtu
keyMyths.description: Pazuzu is often depicted as the enemy of Lamashtu, a female demon who threatened pregnant women and infants. Amulets featuring Pazuzu were used to ward off Lamashtu's evil influence.
keyMyths.source: Black and Green, Gods, Demons, and Symbols of Ancient Mesopotamia

keyMyths.title: Son of Hanbi
keyMyths.description: Pazuzu is the son of the god Hanbi, a relatively obscure deity. This lineage connects him to the divine realm, despite his demonic nature and role.
keyMyths.source: Jeremy Black and Anthony Green, Gods, Demons and Symbols of Ancient Mesopotamia

keyMyths.title: Controller of the Winds
keyMyths.description: Pazuzu's power resides in his control over the winds, both the destructive and the protective. This ability made him both feared and revered in ancient Mesopotamia.

sources.author: Jeremy Black and Anthony Green
sources.work: Gods, Demons and Symbols of Ancient Mesopotamia
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Mesopotamian deities and supernatural beings.
sources.text: A comprehensive overview of Mesopotamian deities and supernatural beings.

sources.author: Stephanie Dalley
sources.description: A collection of translated Mesopotamian myths.
sources.text: A collection of translated Mesopotamian myths.

cultural.modernLegacy: Pazuzu has experienced a resurgence in popular culture, appearing in horror films like 'The Exorcist,' literature, and art. This modern fascination often focuses on his terrifying image and association with demonic forces, sometimes overshadowing his protective aspects in ancient Mesopotamian beliefs. His image is used in games, comics, and other media.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Pazuzu's origins are rooted in ancient Mesopotamian demonology. He is identified as the son of Hanbi, a somewhat obscure deity possibly associated with the underworld or chaos. Unlike many demons who arise from primordial chaos, Pazuzu's lineage connects him, albeit tenuously, to the divine realm. The specific circumstances of his birth are not detailed in surviving texts, but his parentage suggests a complex relationship between the gods and the demonic. His emergence reflects the Mesopotamian worldview where deities and demons were intertwined, and even malevolent entities could serve a purpose within the cosmic order. Pazuzu's very existence highlights the blurred lines between good and evil in ancient Mesopotamian thought.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: While Pazuzu does not feature prominently in elaborate narratives like some other Mesopotamian deities, his significance lies in his apotropaic role. He is primarily known for his power to ward off other malevolent forces, particularly the demoness Lamashtu, who threatened pregnant women and infants. Amulets and statues of Pazuzu were often placed in homes to protect against her influence. There are no surviving myths detailing grand battles or elaborate stories of Pazuzu's exploits, but his image and inscriptions on amulets attest to his crucial function as a protector. In this context, Pazuzu's myth is less about specific events and more about his constant vigilance against other malevolent forces, ensuring the safety and well-being of individuals and communities.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Direct worship of Pazuzu, in the sense of temples or dedicated rituals, is not well-documented. Instead, his 'cult' consisted primarily of the creation and use of amulets and figurines. These objects, often inscribed with protective spells and incantations, were believed to harness Pazuzu's power to ward off evil. They were strategically placed in homes, particularly in bedrooms and nurseries, to safeguard against Lamashtu and other harmful spirits. The effectiveness of these objects depended on the proper ritual performance by exorcists or priests. While not a deity receiving regular offerings or sacrifices, Pazuzu was nonetheless an important figure in Mesopotamian religious practice, representing a crucial line of defense against the unseen forces that threatened daily life. His presence was invoked through ritual and object, not through formalized worship.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Pazuzu's legacy extends far beyond ancient Mesopotamia. His striking imagery, particularly his grotesque and composite form, has captured the imagination of artists and writers for centuries. In modern times, he is most famously known for his appearance in the film 'The Exorcist,' where his image became synonymous with demonic possession. While this portrayal often emphasizes his malevolent aspects, it has also sparked renewed interest in Mesopotamian mythology and demonology. His image continues to appear in popular culture, from video games to literature, often as a symbol of both terror and protection, reflecting the complex duality of his ancient role. Despite his fearsome appearance, Pazuzu remains a testament to the Mesopotamian belief in the power of demons to both harm and protect.

powers: Protection against evil spirits and diseases
shortDescription: Pazuzu is an Assyrian and Babylonian demon king of the wind, known for his terrifying appearance and role as both a bringer of and protector against evil.
